text: The surgeon performs limited adhesiolysis, focusing on the small bowel, while the colon remains visible but unmobilized in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon along with the peritoneum en bloc, performing a limited dissection to ensure no structures are missed.
The surgeon lyses adhesions between the omentum and the sidewall, preparing to initiate a preperitoneal dissection, commonly performed for lateral hernias.

The surgeon performs a combination of preperitoneal and pre-transversalis dissection, revealing a healthy and robust preperitoneal fat pad that aids in the straightforward separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon dissects towards the side wall, revealing the transversus abdominis muscle and its underlying transversalis fascia.
The surgeon begins the reduction of the hernia sac, utilizing the previously developed spaces on either side for access.
The surgeon removes adhesions and identifies the pseudosac, pushing it away from the hernia sac, revealing a prominent volcano sign in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the hernia neck, removing straightforward adhesions. As the procedure progresses into the retroperitoneum, the kidney is mobilized.
The surgeon encounters adhesions to the retroperitoneal fat and proceeds to develop the dissection beyond the defect margins to ensure broad mesh coverage.

The surgeon performs subdiaphragmatic dissection near the twelfth rib, reflecting the diaphragm upwards and away from the peritoneum. Some diaphragmatic fibers are visible being pulled down during the procedure.

The surgeon continues to develop the lateral dissection space, aiming for broad mesh coverage. The retroperitoneal muscular anatomy becomes visible, including the twelfth rib. Adhesions from a prior operation are carefully dissected and removed.
The surgeon exposes portions of the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscle on the right side of the screen, along with the transversus abdominis. Some neuroanatomy is visible in the surgical field.

The surgical view highlights the quadratus lumborum muscle, transversus abdominis, and the twelfth rib. Various nerves are visible, including the subcostal nerve, iliohypogastric nerve, and ilioinguinal nerve.
Following thorough dissection, the surgeon prepares for defect closure using a number one non-absorbable suture. The edges of the lumbar hernia defect are re-approximated in a transverse orientation.

The surgeon applies sequential tightening and traction to facilitate closure of the lumbar region, which had a full thickness injury through the fascia, while the more central areas exhibited a partial thickness hernia.
The surgeon positions a heavyweight mesh over the lumbar hernia defect, ensuring it covers the area adequately. The mesh is carefully placed to provide support and stability to the repaired region.

The surgeon introduces a twelve millimeter laparoscopic trocar centrally within the dissection area to facilitate the placement of larger pieces of heavyweight mesh. This approach aims to cover the area with mesh to minimize the risk of incisional hernia associated with larger trocars.

The heavyweight mesh is already positioned over the lumbar hernia site, with the kidney visibly reflected throughout the procedure. The surgeon prepares for standard flap closure using a two-oh absorbable v-lock suture.
